coredata.dc:title: A novel g-C3N4/tourmaline composites equipped with plasmonic MoO3−x to boost photocatalytic activity

Dong, Faqin
Lv, Zhenzhen
Li, Hailong
Zhou, Lin
Chen, Yuheng
Huo, Tingting
Luo, Xijie
coredata.dc:description: A novel ternary MoO3−x/g-C3N4/Tourmaline photocatalyst was synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method through anchoring MoO3−x on g-C3N4, which was coated on the surface of tourmaline. From the analysis to physicochemical, photoelectric and electronic structures properties of the obtained samples, the spontaneous surface electric fields of tourmaline promoted the spatial separation of the photogenerated e−/h+ pairs, and the abundant oxygen vacancies of MoO3−x contributed to the plasmon resonance, endowing MoO3−x/g-C3N4/Tourmaline with stronger and wider light absorption ability. Benefiting from the synergistic effect of the surface spontaneous polarization electric fields of tourmaline and the LSPR effect of MoO3−x, MoO3−x/g-C3N4/Tourmaline achieved a remarkably improved photodegradation rate for RhB, presenting almost 100% removal efficiency under visible light irradiation within 40 min. Recycling experiments presented that MoO3−x/g-C3N4/Tourmaline exhibited excellent stability and recoverability. Moreover, according to the trapping experiments, h+ and •O2 − were the dominant active species and a possible reaction mechanism was proposed.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel ternary MoO3−x/g-C3N4/Tourmaline photocatalyst was synthesized by a facile hydrothermal method through anchoring MoO3−x on g-C3N4, which was coated on the surface of tourmaline. From the analysis to physicochemical, photoelectric and electronic structures properties of the obtained samples, the spontaneous surface electric fields of tourmaline promoted the spatial separation of the photogenerated e−/h+ pairs, and the abundant oxygen vacancies of MoO3−x contributed to the plasmon resonance, endowing MoO3−x/g-C3N4/Tourmaline with stronger and wider light absorption ability. Benefiting from the synergistic effect of the surface spontaneous polarization electric fields of tourmaline and the LSPR effect of MoO3−x, MoO3−x/g-C3N4/Tourmaline achieved a remarkably improved photodegradation rate for RhB, presenting almost 100% removal efficiency under visible light irradiation within 40 min. Recycling experiments presented that MoO3−x/g-C3N4/Tourmaline exhibited excellent stability and recoverability. Moreover, according to the trapping experiments, h+ and •O2 − were the dominant active species and a possible reaction mechanism was proposed.

ce:section.ce:para: With rapid industrialization, water pollution has been recognized as an enormous challenge to human health [1]. In waste water system, organic dyes are the main origin due to the toxicity and stability [2,3]. Photocatalysis, a ‘green’ and cost-effective route, has been widely studied for organic pollutants elimination [4]. Graphitic carbon nitride (g-C3N4), a metal-free polymeric semiconductor, is widely used as a photocatalyst [5,6]. It has garnered special attention due to its unique two-dimensional graphene-like structure, nontoxicity, excellent chemical stability, moderate band gap (2.7 eV), low cost, excellent adsorption capacity, and ability to decompose organic pollutants [6]. Nevertheless, photoactivity of g-C3N4 is limited due to the particularly rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers and insufficient visible light utilization [7]. To overcome these imperfections, many modified g-C3N4-based photocatalysts are applied to kinds of researches recently, such as defect-modified construction [8], doping with metal [9] or nonmetal elements [10,11], fabricating heterojunction with other semiconductors [12–14] and changing morphology [15]. Among them, constructing composite photocatalysts between g-C3N4 and other semiconductors are considered to be one of the most effective methods in the photocatalytic field. The g-C3N4-based heterostructures can not only suppress the recombination of photogenerated charge carriers, but also endow the photocatalysts with some novel features due to a synergistic effect between g-C3N4 and other components in heterostructures [13,14]. Hence, it is meaningful to develop novel g-C3N4-based composites to enhance the electron transportation and expand the light absorption range simultaneously.

Molybdenum trioxide (MoO3), as a visible light-excited semiconductor, has been frequently selected to improve the activity of g-C3N4 [2,16]. To date, the excellent photocatalytic activities of several MoO3/g-C3N4 heterostructures for dye degradation have been reported. However, the photoactivity of MoO3/g-C3N4 composite is restricted due to the defect-free MoO3: (1) poor absorption ability for visible light (band gap = 2.80–3.10 eV) [2,14,16,17]; (2) low electrical conductivity, hindering electron transport rate in the composite. To resolve this problem, the method of introducing oxygen vacancy has been proved to be effective in enhancing the photocatalytic properties of MoO3−x. Compare with MoO3, MoO3−x with oxygen vacancy not only displays localized surface plasmon resonance (LSPR) absorption in a wide spectral range [8,18], but also possesses a narrow band gap and better conductivity [19,20]. LSPR is a near-field enhancement phenomenon that can maximize the light absorption capacity of photocatalysts and broaden the light absorption range [21]. Besides, as a non-noble metal semiconductor plasmonic material, MoO3−x in the composite can generate high energy hot electrons to participate in the redox reaction [22]. Furthermore, the introduction of oxygen vacancy leads to the formation of Z-type heterojunction between MoO3−x and g-C3N4, facilitating the separation efficiency of e−/h+ pairs [8]. Currently, synthetic approaches of MoO3−x with oxygen vacancy included chemical reduction method [22], ion intercalation [23] and CVD [24]. Among these methods, the chemical reduction method with ethanol as reducing agent has attracted the attention of researchers in recent years due to the advantages of facile, environment-friendly, mild and low cost [20].Therefore, we believe that photocatalysts with the LSPR effect and oxygen vacancy are desirable for achieving higher photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants. Nonetheless, the drawback of the rapid recombination rate of plasma-induced carriers remains challenging. Fortunately, photocatalytic materials with spontaneous polarization effect have been proved to be able to effectively inhibit the recombination of e−/h+ pairs, and widely used to improve the efficiency of photocatalysis [25–27]. The spontaneously polarized materials can generate an internal electric field, which can promote the movement of photogenerated carriers in the opposite direction and even achieve the spatial separation of redox sites [25]. Recently, some reports have focused on the enhancement of photocatalytic activity by polarized semiconductors (e.g., ferroelectrics, such as PbTiO3 [26], LiNbO3 [28], BiFeO3 [29]). However, the high cost and the tedious preparation process of ferroelectric materials limit their large-scale application. Further efforts are needed to find a material with low cost, abundant resources, convenient synthesis and excellent photochemical properties. Tourmaline, as a polar natural mineral, is widely and abundantly distributed in the earth's crust [30]. Obviously different from other minerals, tourmaline has fascinating properties analogous to ferroelectrics and exhibits a unique behavior with spontaneous and permanent poles. Internal dipolar field presents in the surface of tourmaline with an intensity of 106–107 V/m, which arises from the spontaneous polarization due to lack of symmetric center or rotation axes oriented perpendicular to the c axis in the crystal [31–33]. Tourmaline can inhibit the recombination of photogenerated e−/h+ pairs in the catalyst due to its surface spontaneous polarization electric field, prolonging the life-times of the charge carriers and improving the photodegradation activity [34,35]. Also, the far-infrared radiation of tourmaline can reduce the cluster effect of water molecules caused by hydrogen bonds to increase the oxygen dissolved in water, which is also beneficial to the photodegradation reaction [21,36]. Therefore, tourmaline is often used as a co-catalyst for photocatalysis, achieving favorable effects in the fields of air purification [36] and sewage treatment [30,34]. However, to our knowledge, there has not been any report on the synergistic enhancement of photocatalytic properties obtained by the surface spontaneous polarization electric field of tourmaline and the LSPR effect of MoO3−x.

Herein, a novel catalyst MoO3−x/g-C3N4/Tourmaline is prepared by using a simple and facile one-step hydrothermal method to coat the surface of tourmaline with g-C3N4 and anchor plasmonic MoO3−x into g-C3N4. As expected, the as-prepared catalyst presents superior photocatalytic activities for the degradation of Rhodamine B (RhB) under visible light owing to the synergistic effect of the surface spontaneous polarization electric field of tourmaline and the LSPR effect of MoO3−x. The tight heterojunction formed between g-C3N4 and MoO3−x can further accelerate the separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers. In addition, the similar core-shell structure of M3−xCT-20 can not only fully utilize the surface electric field of the tourmaline, but also avoid weakening or canceling of the surface spontaneous polarization electric field due to the direct contact between tourmalines. Finally, the inherent abundant pore structure and large specific surface area of MoO3−x/g-C3N4/Tourmaline catalyst are also beneficial to enhance the photocatalytic degradation ability. In order to explain its excellent photocatalytic performance, morphology, specific surface area, pore distribution, valence band structure, optical characteristics, electrical characteristics and stability of the as-prepared composite catalysts are discussed. Finally, the synergistic enhancement mechanism of photocatalytic performance by surface plasmon resonance effect and spontaneous polarization effect is proposed.

MoO3 powder, urea, ethanol, and Rhodamine B (RhB) were purchased from ChengDu KeLong Chemical Co., Ltd., China. Natural tourmaline powders (D50 = 10 μm) from the Xinjiang Uygur Autonomous Region of China, whose chemical element compositions were analyzed by X-Ray Fluorescence (XRF) and given in the weight percentage as follows: SiO2 45.46%, MgO 7.82%, Al2O3 33.49%, Fe2O3 8.71%, Na2O 2.17%, CaO 1.34% and others 1.01%. All reagents were analytically pure without any further purification. Deionized water was used in the whole experiment.

Procedures used in the preparation of g-C3N4 have been reported previously [2]. Briefly, 5 g urea was heated to 550 °C with a heating rate of 10 °C min−1 and maintained for 4 h in a muffle furnace. The calcination process was in a covered crucible to prevent sublimation, and then the yellow powder (g-C3N4) was obtained.

The MoO3−x/g-C3N4/Tourmaline (M3−xCT) composite was prepared via a solvothermal process (Fig. 1 ). Typically, 300 mg g-C3N4 was ultrasonically dispersed in 15 mL ethanol, then optimal amount of MoO3 (60 mg) was added, and with continuous ultrasonic for 60 min. Subsequently, the mixture was continuous ultrasonic for 60 min with the addition of appropriate of tourmaline. The varying mass of tourmaline was 5, 10, 20 and 30 mg. Then, the suspension was poured into a 25 mL of Teflon-lined stainless steel autoclave, being heated at 140 °C for 12 h. After being cool down to room temperature (25 °C), the sediment was acquired by centrifugation and then washed with distilled water and absolute ethanol in turn for several times. Finally, the required composite material was obtained after being dried at 60 °C for 24 h in a stove. The MoO3−x/g-C3N4/tourmaline composite was noted as M3−xCT-X, where X refers to the mass of tourmaline in the composite.

MoO3−x/g-C3N4 (M3−xC) without tourmaline, MoO3−x/tourmaline (M3−xT) without g-C3N4 and tourmaline/g-C3N4 (CT) without MoO3 were prepared according to the M3−xCT preparation process, respectively. The fully oxide MoO3/g-C3N4/tourmaline (MCT) composite was prepared in the same way with M3−xCT composite using water as solvent instead of ethanol. Photographs of synthesized materials are shown in Fig. S1.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the catalysts were recorded on a PANalytical X'pert PRO diffractometer equipped with CuKa radiation at a scanning rate of 2°/min. The morphology, microstructure and elemental composition of the catalysts were examined using a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2010, JEOL, Japan) and scanning electron microscopy with energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (SEM-EDX, ZEISS EVO@18, Germany). The morphology and surface potential (SP) of the catalysts were measured with a AFM (AFM-KFM, SPA-300 HV, Japan) using the KFM imaging mode. The surface area, pore size and pore volume of the catalysts were measured by using Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) nitrogen adsorption isotherm method (Nova 3000, USA). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) of the sample were obtained on a Nicolet 5700 FTIR spectrometer (PE Instrument Co., USA) in the wavenumber range of 4000–450 cm−1 with KBr pellet. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) spectra were obtained on AXIS Ultra DLD X-ray photoelectron spectroscope (XPS, Kratos Analytical Ltd., UK) using Al Kα (hυ = 1486.68 eV). The absorption performance was analyzed through a UV–vis diffuse reflection spectroscopy (UV–vis DRS, Solidspec-3700, Shimadzu, Japan) with BaSO4 as the background. The photoluminescence (PL) spectra of catalysts were obtained on an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan) at room temperature.

Photocurrent and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) were tested via the electrochemical analyzer (CHI 660E, Chenhua), which was in a standard three-electrode system, using Pt wire as counter electrode, Hg/Hg2Cl2 electrode as reference electrode and 300 W xenon lamp as light source. Additionally, the work electrode was prepared as follows: 5 mg of sample were dispersed in mixed solvents of deionized water (625 μL) and ethanol (625 μL) by 30 min sonication. Then, 25 μL 5 wt% Nafion solutions was added into the dispersed solution and further sonicated for 30 min. Finally, the suspension was coated on a 1 × 3 cm conductive glass (FTO) and dried at 65 °C for 6 h. Moreover, 0.5 M Na2SO4 was mainly employed as the electrolyte for electrochemical and photoelectrochemical measurements.

The photocatalytic efficiency of catalysts was tested via photo-degrading RhB solution under visible light illumination. A 300 W xenon lamp was taken as visible light source. 50 mg of as-prepared catalyst was mixed with 100 mL of RhB solution (10 mg•L−1). Before experiment, the mixtures were stirred for 0.5 h in the dark to reach absorption - desorption equilibrium. At certain time intervals, 5 mL of the suspension was collected and then centrifuged to remove the photocatalyst. The real-time concentrations of RhB were determined by a UV–vis spectrophotometer (UV-2550, Shimadzu, Japan) at the wavelength of 554 nm. The TOC was also measured with a Shimadzu, TOC-Vcph analyzer. After degradation experiment, the photocatalyst collected by centrifugation was washed twice with ethanol, and then dried at 60 °C. The degradation experiment was repeated and five times to test the stability of the sample. Meanwhile, in the trapping experiment, isopropanol (IPA), ammonium oxalate (AO) and 1, 4-benzoquinone (p-BQ) were used as quenchers of hydroxyl radicals (•OH), holes (h+) and superoxide radical (•O2 −), respectively.

Fig. 2a shows XRD patterns of as-prepared samples. The diffraction peaks of pure g-C3N4 appearing at 12.7° and 27.5° correspond to the (100) and (002) planes, which are assigned to the inter-layer structural packing and the inter-planar stacking peaks of aromatic systems, respectively [2]. After hybridized with MoO3−x, the diffraction peaks of the M3−xC show the primary characteristic peaks of both materials, confirming the coexistence of g-C3N4 and MoO3−x in the M3−xC composites. As for tourmaline, its XRD pattern owns a series of sharp and narrow diffraction peaks, which is in agreement with JCPDS No.44-1457 and indicating that the natural tourmaline possess good crystallinity. After coating g-C3N4 on the surface of tourmaline, all the peaks of CT composites match well with those of g-C3N4 and tourmaline with no detection of impurities. Further, when g-C3N4, MoO3−x and tourmaline hybrids form ternary catalysts, the characteristic peaks of these materials can also be observed in M3−xCT-20 composites. Moreover, MoO3−x in the M3−xCT-20 shows stronger characteristic peaks compared with the M3−xC, showing an increasing crystallinity of MoO3−x after incorporated into tourmaline. Briefly, the above results of XRD indicate that the M3−xC, CT and M3−xCT-20 composites have been successfully synthesized. Additionally, the crystal structure and peak position of the catalyst have no obvious change in all samples, implying that the main phase of the catalyst can be maintained.

The FTIR spectra of the synthesized samples are shown in Fig. 2b. Characteristic peaks of g-C3N4 are present in the range of 1750–800 cm−1: the strong peaks at 1750–1000 cm−1 are associated with aromatic of CN stretching and the absorption peak located at 810 cm−1 is assigned to the triazine units [2,20]. For MoO3−x, the absorption peaks at 559, 820 and 981 cm−1 are clearly observed, which are assigned to MoOMo and MoO [8,20]. Obviously, the typical peaks of MoO3−x and g-C3N4 are commonly presented in the M3−xC samples, suggesting that the M3−xC heterojunction is fabricated successfully. However, in M3−xCT-20 composites, no additional peaks related to tourmaline can be detected, because the characteristic peak of the tourmaline is covered by the M3−xC partial peak. But, compared with M3−xCT-20 and M3−xC, the strength of the peak at 710 and 958 cm−1 is obviously enhanced by the addition of tourmaline [30]. Thus, the FTIR results further indicate that the M3−xC and M3−xCT-20 composites has been successfully synthesized.

The morphology and microstructure information of the as-prepared samples were investigated by SEM and TEM. As shown in Fig. S2a and inset of Fig. S2a, the prepared g-C3N4 is composed of thin, wrinkle-enriched and continuous nanosheets. Besides, it can be clearly found that there are a lot of cracks and micropores on the surface of g-C3N4. The tourmaline samples exhibits block-shaped particles with a size of about 10 μm (Fig. S2b). Furthermore, Atomic force microscope (AFM) and Kelvin force microscopy (KFM) was employed to probe the morphology and the surface potential (SP) on the surfaces of the tourmaline (Fig. S3). Fig. S3a is an Atomic force microscope (AFM) morphological image of tourmaline. It can be seen that tourmaline has a rough surface with a height difference of about 10–20 nm. Fig. S3b shows the SP distribution of tourmaline. KFM results show that tourmaline surface has a large SP (average SP of 4.45 × 103 mV), and the SP is different due to the difference of surface height (3.40–5.53 × 103 mV). From the mentioned results, it can be inferred that there is an electric field on the surface of tourmaline [27,37]. For MoO3−x, it presents a rod or belt structure with a width of 0.5–2.0 μm and a length of 2.0–4.0 μm (Fig. S2c). After coupling MoO3−x and g-C3N4, the MoO3−x are randomly scattered over the g-C3N4 sheets to configure heterostructure (Fig. S2d). Interestingly, after coupling tourmaline and g-C3N4, g-C3N4 sheets were widely distributed on the surface of the tourmaline particles, which resulted in the formation of a ellipsoid-like structure (Fig. S2e). For ternary M3−xCT-20 composite (Fig. 3a), it can be observed that M3−xCT-20 is elliptical with diameters of 20–40 μm, surrounded by few fragments of g-C3N4. The surface of M3−xCT-20 presents a rough, porous, wrinkled and fissured structure due to tourmaline loosely wrapped by g-C3N4. Meanwhile, it can be clearly observed that rod-like or banded MoO3−x is anchored on g-C3N4, forming a heterojunction structure on the M3−xCT-20 ellipsoid (Fig. 3b). Yet, tourmaline cannot be found in SEM because it is completely wrapped by g-C3N4. In fact, MoO3−x, g-C3N4 and tourmaline are clearly observed in the TEM image of M3−xCT-20, and they form ternary heterojunction via physical junction. Additionally, the results of EDX (Fig. S2f) and corresponding elemental mapping images of the selected region indicated in Fig. 3b exhibit that the M3−xCT-20 composite contains elements of C, N, O, Mo, Si, Al, Fe and Mg (Fig. 3d–k). The existence and uniform distribution of these elements provides strong evidence for the successful preparation of M3−xCT-20 ternary composites.

Surface chemistry and valence band structure of the synthesized samples were investigated by using XPS. The survey scan spectra of synthesized materials are shown in Fig. 4a. Obviously, g-C3N4 contains C and N element, and the other three composites contains the element of C, N, O and Mo. However, in all the samples, no additional peaks related to tourmaline can be detected, which might be due to small amount of tourmaline below the detection limit. The high-resolution XPS spectrum of C1s is observed in Fig. 4b, and the binding energy centered at 287.9 eV is assigned to the sp2-hybridized carbon in N-containing aromatic ring (NCN) [13]. In terms of N1s, as shown in Fig. 4c, four peaks are present in each of these catalysts. The main peaks at 398.4 eV, 399.8 and 400.9 eV are from the CNC, N(C)3 and NH2 groups, respectively. And meanwhile, the small one at 404.0 eV is from the ππ* satellite and NO3 − [11]. Oxidation state and surface oxygen vacancy can be detected by Mo3d and O1s spectra. O1s spectra are shown in Fig. 4d, for the MCT-20 sample, the peak at 531.2 eV is attributed to the lattice oxygen (defined as Olatt) in MoO3 [14]. For the other two composites, two peaks of O1s spectra are observed at 530.3 and 532.1 eV, respectively. The peak located at 530.3 eV can be assigned to Olatt, while the peak centered at approximately 532.1 eV is ascribed to surface adsorbed oxygen (defined as Oads) derived from surface hydroxyl groups and oxygen vacancy [39,40]. Compared to the MCT-20 sample, a shift of the Olatt peak towards lower binding energy can be found on M3−xC and M3−xCT-20. The lower binding energy of Olatt can make the surface oxygen more unstable, which is favorable for the catalytic degradation of dye and intermediates adsorbed on the catalyst surface [38,40]. Moreover, the peak area ratio of Oads/(Oads + Olatt) on the surface of M3−xCT-20 samples is obviously higher than that on the M3−xC surface due to the tourmaline modification. It is generally believed that the presence of oxygen vacancy greatly favors the generation of Oads on the catalyst surface, which is propitious to catalytic oxidation reaction due to its higher mobility than Olatt [38,39]. Furthermore, in MCT-20 sample, the Mo3d spectrum (Fig. 4e) shows two typical peaks at 231.8 and 234.9 eV. These peaks are corresponding to Mo3d5/2 and Mo3d3/2 for Mo6+ in MoO3 [14,16]. However, for M3−xC and M3−xCT-20 composites, there are two doublets at the binding energies of 235.4/232.3 eV and 234.1/231.1 eV, respectively [2]. The first doublet at 235.4/232.3 eV corresponds to Mo6+, while the latter is ascribed to Mo5+ species. Moreover, it is also seen that the M3−xCT-20 samples show higher Mo5+/(Mo5+ + Mo6+) ratio compared to the M3−xC sample. This phenomenon may also be caused by tourmaline modification. It is well recognized that higher relative content of Mo5+ can result in generating more oxygen vacancies due to charge compensation, which in turn favors the destruction of dye and intermediate molecules [20,22,38]. Therefore, the XPS results demonstrate that oxygen vacancy is successfully introduced to M3−xCT-20 composite, and tourmaline modification is beneficial to the generation of oxygen vacancies.

The absorption properties and the optical bandgap of the as-prepared photocatalysts were evaluated by UV–vis DRS. It is observed from Fig. 5a that all the samples show strong absorption in the ultraviolet region, but weak and wide absorption in visible light, and simultaneously each sample exhibits the different absorption profile. For MoO3, g-C3N4 and tourmaline, the optical absorption edge was estimated to be about 420, 450 and 410 nm, respectively. Compared with MoO3, the absorption edge of MoO3−x exhibits an obvious red-shift from 420 to 460 nm, due to the LSPR effect caused by introduction of oxygen vacancies [3,8,18]. After coupling MoO3−x and g-C3N4, the absorption edge gradually moved to longer edge, due to the forming heterojunction between two semiconductors [8]. Moreover, the ternary nanocomposite of M3−xCT-20 showed a significant red shift of the absorption edges as compared with all other samples, thus synergetic effect in the ternary system worked. Also, as displayed in Fig. 5b, the optical absorption edge of M3−xCT-X has a red-shifted followed by a blue-shift as increasing tourmaline contents, and reaches a maximum of 475 nm at 20 mg tourmaline loading. This may be due to the fact that the separation and migration of e−/h+ pairs in g-C3N4 and MoO3−x can be enhanced by the electrostatic field on the surface of tourmaline, as reported in previous researches [31,34,41–43]. Additionally, it can also be seen from Fig. 5a that the catalyst containing MoO3−x not only has a red-shift on the optical absorption edges, but also significantly increases the absorption intensity in the visible region. This phenomenon further corroborated the MoO3−x with LSPR effect can effectively enhance the absorption capacity of the catalyst to visible light [18].

In summary, UV–vis DRS spectra results show that the combined result of the surface spontaneous polarization electric field of tourmaline and the LSPR effect of MoO3−x provides a wider and stronger light absorption capability, which is expected to enhance photocatalyst activity.

Fig. 5d displayed the photoluminescence spectra (PL) of as-obtained samples under the excitation wavelength of 300 nm at room temperature. As known that, PL spectroscopy is usually used to analyze the efficiency of charge carrier transfers, trapping and separation of the photogenerated e−/h+ pairs in composite [48]. As seen in the figure, all synthesized samples display the emission peak around 460 nm, which were consistent with the UV–vis DRS in Fig. 5a. For the g-C3N4, the PL intensity is the highest among all the samples, indicating that the photogenerated e−/h+ pairs recombine easily and dissipate the input energy as heat [49]. After coupling with MoO3−x, the intensities of the PL spectra decreased, suggesting that the recombination of photogenerated e−/h+ pairs was greatly restrained due to the rapid charge separation and transfer when the M3−xC heterojuction was formed [8]. Compared to M3−xC, the PL intensity of the M3−xCT-X is lower, and the M3−xCT-20 possesses the lowest PL intensity. The PL intensities of as-obtained samples obeyed the sequence of M3−xCT-20 < M3−xCT-30 < M3−xCT-10 < MCT-20 < M3−xCT-5 < M3−xC < g-C3N4, implying that the surface spontaneous polarization electric field of tourmaline can inhibit the recombination of photogenerated e−/h+ pairs [35]. Nevertheless, excess tourmaline increases the PL intensity of the M3−xCT-x catalyst. It may be caused by the strong interaction between tourmaline particles due to the large amount of tourmaline, which weakens or cancels the surface spontaneous polarization electric field between each other, resulting in the low efficiency of separation of e−/h+ pairs. Furthermore, compared to M3−xCT-20, the PL intensity of the MCT-20 is higher, further indicating that MoO3−x with LSPR effect can effectively promote the separation of e−/h+ pairs [8]. Consequently, PL spectra results show that the synergistic effect of tourmaline and MoO3−x can promote the effective separation and transfer of g-C3N4 e−/h+ pairs, coinciding with the results of UV–vis DRS spectra.

To further clarify the separation and transfer feature of the photogenerated e−/h+ pairs, the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and the photocurrent response tests of as-prepared g-C3N4, M3−xC, MCT-20 and M3−xCT-20 were measured and displayed in Fig. 6 . In general, the higher photocurrent intensity and the smaller arc radius in an EIS suggest the higher charge carriers separation rate and transfer efficiency [35]. Fig. 6a shows the photocurrent response curves of the samples with four on-off cycles of intermittent irradiation. It can be seen that the pure g-C3N4 exhibits the lowest photocurrent, and M3−xCT-20 has evidently the larger photocurrent compared to the others. In addition, as shown in Fig. S5, the photocurrent density of MoO3−x is about two times higher than that of MoO3 due to the hot electrons derived from the LSPR excitation of MoO3−x under visible light irradiation [8,21]. Similarly, the results of EIS in Fig. 6b are well in accordance with photocurrent response results, and the relative arc sizes of the as-prepared samples obey the order of g-C3N4 > M3−xC > MCT-20 > M3−xCT-20. The above results confirm that the M3−xCT-20 sample exhibits better interfacial charge transfer and higher efficiency in separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers after the introduction of MoO3−x and tourmaline in the g-C3N4 system, which promote the improvement of photocatalytic performance. Indeed, the photoelectrochemical analyses result is consistent with the conclusions obtained from PL and the photocatalytic activity for RhB degradation which is confirmed in the following photocatalytic activity tests.

The photocatalytic performance and decomposition efficiency of all samples were evaluated using a typical organic dye RhB. Fig. 7a displays the temporal evolution of RhB concentration in the presence of the as-prepared photocatalysts. Before exposed to the light irradiation, the reaction system was placed under dark conditions and continuously stirred for 30 min to establish the adsorption-desorption equilibrium. The blank experiments displayed that the self-degradation and photodegradation of RhB is negligible under dark and visible light illumination. Tourmaline, MoO3−x and M3−xT show a weak adsorption capacity, with the adsorption efficiency of RhB less than 10% within 30 min under dark. Conversely, g-C3N4 based catalysts has a stronger adsorption capacity for RhB under dark conditions due to the larger specific surface area of g-C3N4 [50]. The adsorption capacity of g-C3N4 based catalyst is different due to the different specific surface area, which is consistent with the specific surface test results (Fig. S4). Besides, the adsorption of RhB by g-C3N4 based catalysts without light is fast, and the equilibrium is achieved within 5 min, without obvious decrease in the following 25 min. The reason may be that the inherent rich pore structures and large specific surface area of g-C3N4 promote the transfer and diffusion of reactant and product molecules in the solution, accelerating the process of adsorption-desorption reaction [51]. It can be predicted that because the surface adsorption of reactants on photocatalysts is the key factor affecting the photocatalytic efficiency, the catalyst with rapid and strong adsorption ability will have excellent photocatalytic performance [52]. When irradiated under visible light, the photocatalysis of tourmaline for RhB shows the negligible performance due to the low specific surface area and poor photocatalytic activity of tourmaline. Pure g-C3N4 and MoO3−x show a limited degradation effect after 40 min photoreaction, and their degradation efficiencies are 60.30% and 53.20%, respectively. The poor photocatalytic performance of g-C3N4 can be ascribed to the rapid recombination of photogenerated charge carriers and insufficient visible light utilization [7]. For MoO3−x, its limited photocatalytic performance is due to the lower specific surface area and low affinity for RhB [3]. Compared with g-C3N4 and MoO3−x, the photocatalytic activity of CT and M3−xC composites has been increased effectively. Conversely, the photocatalytic activity of M3−xT composites is slightly reduced compared with pure MoO3−x due to the decrease of specific surface area. Obviously, compared with single-component and two-component samples, the ternary composite of M3−xCT-X sample exhibited a remarkably enhanced photocatalytic performance. Almost 100% of RhB was photodegraded after 40 min of visible light illumination in the presence of the M3−xCT-20 sample. It is due to the synergistic effect of polar tourmaline and plasmonic MoO3−x, which enhance the light capture ability of the photocatalyst, as well as the carrier separation and transfer efficiency of the photocatalyst, so as to enhance the photocatalytic activity in RhB photodegradation. It is noteworthy that the photocatalytic activity of M3−xCT-X composites has a rise followed by a decline as increasing tourmaline contents, and reaches a maximum at 20 mg tourmaline loading, coinciding with the results of UV–vis DRS spectra. In addition, the photocatalytic performance of MCT-20 sample is lower than that of M3−xC sample and M3−xCT-X sample. Overall, the photocatalytic activity test results is consistent with the conclusions obtained from PL and DRS, and the M3−xCT-20 composite is also superior to the recently reported photocatalysts in terms of activity towards RhB (Table S2). Furthermore, to quantitatively analyze the catalytic efficiency, the photodegration curves of RhB were fitted by pseudo-first-order reaction kinetics. The corresponding reaction rate constants (k) were shown in Fig. 7b. It can be seen that the ternary composites of M3−xCT-x sample have higher rate constants than those of single-component and two-component samples, which are in accord with the PL results. The M3−xCT-20 product exhibits the highest rate constant of 0.1123 min−1 and the highest RhB degradation rate of 100%, and its rate constant is more than 2 times that of other catalysts.

Furthermore, to further disclose the mineralization efficiency of RhB in the photocatalytic process by photocatalysts, the total organic carbon (TOC) was evaluated. From Fig. 7c, it could be observed that, after visible light irradiation for 40 min, the mineralization efficiencies of M3−xCT-20, g-C3N4, M3−xC and MCT-20 were calculated to be 59.48%, 29.31%, 40.85% and 36.23%, respectively. The above results indicated that the M3−xCT-20 could not only degrade RhB but also mineralize it under visible light irradiation, working as an efficient photocatalyst to degrade dye pollutants in water. Subsequently, the photocatalytic cycle experiments of M3−xCT-20 were carried out to evaluate the optical stability of the catalyst. As shown in Fig. 7d, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of RhB decreased slightly after 5 cycles, indicating that M3−xCT-20 had a relatively high stability and no distinct photocorrosion during the oxidation of RhB.

In conclusion, the excellent photocatalytic performance may be ascribed to the following reasons: (1) the surface spontaneous polarization electric field of tourmaline can inhibit the recombination of photogenerated e−/h+ pairs, (2) the MoO3−x with LSPR effect can effectively enhance the absorption capacity of the catalyst to visible light, (3) formation of compact junctions between g-C3N4 and MoO3−x accelerates the separation and transfer of photogenerated charge carriers, and (4) the intrinsic plentiful pore structures and large specific surface area of M3−xCT-20 can provide methods of transport for reactant and product molecules moving in or out of the catalyst during the whole photocatalytic degradation of RhB.

The radical trapping experiments were carried out to examine the effective active species during the photodegradation process. The isopropanol (IPA), ammonium oxalate (AO) and 1, 4-benzoquinone (p-BQ) were added into the photocatalytic reaction system as scavenger to remove hydroxyl radicals (•OH), holes (h+) and superoxide radical (•O2 −), respectively [18,53]. As shown in Fig. 8a, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of catalyst M3−xCT-20 remained at 96.41% after adding IPA, indicating that •OH made little contribution to the photocatalysis. Contrastively, the photocatalytic activity of M3−xCT-20 was almost completely inhibited (only 6.94% RhB was removed) in the presence of AO, implying that h+ was the predominant active species. Besides, with the introduction of p-BQ, the photocatalytic degradation efficiency of RhB was significantly reduced, but 61.72% of RhB were still degraded, indicating that •O2 − had a strong effect on the degradation. The results reveal that all the radicals •OH, h+ and •O2 − will participate in the degradation of RhB by M3−xCT-20, but the holes (h+) and •O2 − are the dominant active species in the photocatlytic reaction.

Based on the above experimental results and analysis, the possible reaction mechanism of M3−xCT-20 catalysts for RhB degradation under visible light irradiation was proposed and illustrated in Fig. 8b. Step 1, upon light irradiation, the electrons of g-C3N4 and MoO3−x were excited and migrate from EVB to ECB, leaving the holes at EVB. In the process of photoexcited electron transition, the abundant oxygen vacancies on the surface of MoO3−x contributed to the plasmon resonance, endowing M3−xCT-20 with stronger and wider light absorption ability [8,18,21], which was confirmed in PL test. Besides, the LSPR effect induced the production of high energy hot electrons, promoting the photodegradation of RhB [21]. More importantly, the surface spontaneous polarization electric field of tourmaline further promotes the separation of e−/h+ pairs in the g-C3N4 and MoO3−x [34,35]. Step 2, similar to the traditional Z-scheme heterojunction route, the photogenerated electrons in the ECB of MoO3−x can directly transfer and recombine with the photogenerated holes in the EVB of g-C3N4 due to the tight contact interface. Particularly, the oxygen vacancy of MoO3−x can receive the electrons from the ECB of MoO3−x and holes from EVB of g-C3N4, which can be served as a recombination center to keep the charge balance, thus improving the separation efficiency of photoexcited charge carriers [8,20]. Step 3, based on the ECB potential of g-C3N4 (−1.21 V) which is more negative than that of O2/•O2 − potential (−0.33 V vs. NHE), the electrons left at ECB of g-C3N4 react with O2, and then main active species •O2 − is generated. Simultaneously, the remained photogenerated holes in EVB of MoO3−x can oxidize H2O to yield •OH, because its EVB is more positive than the H2O/•OH potential (2.40 V vs. NHE). Nevertheless, the results of radical trapping experiments show that holes are the dominant active species in the photocatlytic reaction. Therefore, few of the holes are used to oxidize water, and most of the holes are directly involved in the degradation of RhB. At this step, the spontaneous surface electric fields of tourmaline not only facilitate the space separation of the photogenerated e−/h+ pairs, prolong the lifetime of the generated charge, but also transfer the trapped charges to contact with reactants [34]. At the same time, tourmaline is capable of increasing the dissolved O2 and promote the reaction of electrons in ECB of g-C3N4 with O2 to generate active species •O2 − [34]. Finally, the active species •OH, h+ and •O2 − produced in the above process, especially h+ and •O2 −, react with RhB and degrade it. Moreover, the intrinsic plentiful pore structures and large specific surface area of M3−xCT-20 can provide methods of transport for reactant and product molecules moving in or out of the catalyst during the whole photocatalytic degradation of RhB, and therefore it can accelerate the process of photocatalytic reaction [51]. Above all, combined all favorable effect mentioned above, M3−xCT-20 provide a much higher RhB degradation efficiency than any other samples.

In summary, M3−xCT-X heterjunction photocatalyst was successfully synthesized by a convenient one-step hydrothermal method through anchoring MoO3−x on g-C3N4, which was coated on the surface of tourmaline. The prepared M3−xCT-X composite exhibits the significantly improved photodegradation rate for the RhB removal, presenting almost 100% removal efficiency under visible light irradiation within 40 min. Recycling experiments present that M3−xCT-x retains excellent photo-stability after five-time recycles. The results of characterization and catalyst performance test reveal that the cooperation of polar tourmaline and plasmonic MoO3−x contributes to a synergy effect on light capture capability, as well as carrier separation and transfer efficiency of photocatalysts, which eventually enhance catalyst activity in RhB photodegradation. Moreover, according to the trapping experiments, h+ and •O2 − are dominant active species in the photodegradation process, and a reasonable mechanism based on the results of UV–vis and catalyst performance of photocatalysts has been proposed. This study provides a new way to develop photocatalysts with strong light absorption, high charge separation efficiency and rapid interfacial redox rate.
